commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix: antml:/mm: namespaced tool-call XML leaks into chat replies (#97750)
target.body:
Closes #97750

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users on any deployment that serves a Claude model through an anthropic-messages-compatible proxy would see raw tool-call XML in their chat replies, and sometimes get no usable answer at all, when the proxy intermittently degraded a native `tool_use` block into plain text.

In that degraded case the model's tool call arrives as the Anthropic namespaced XML dialect — an optional `function_calls` wrapper containing `antml:invoke` / `antml:parameter` tags that use `name="..."` attributes (MiniMax emits the same shape with an `mm:` prefix). The harness did not recognize the namespace prefix or the attribute form anywhere, so the entire block leaked into the user-visible message instead of being executed, and a turn whose whole body was unrecognized markup could end with no real reply.

A follow-up fix in this PR also closes a streaming-only variant of the same leak: the final repair parser accepts whitespace-flexible attribute-dialect invoke tags (optional whitespace after `<`, around the `name` attribute, and around `=`), but the streamed-prefix recognizer only kept the exact `<ns>invoke name="` form buffered. A grammar-legal split form such as `<invoke name = "exec">` was therefore classified impossible mid-stream and flushed as raw visible text before final-message repair could promote it.

## Why This Change Was Made

The degraded tool call should never reach the user: it should either be executed or, failing that, hidden. This change teaches both layers that own that contract about the namespaced attribute dialect, using a closed namespace allow-list (`antml:`, `mm:`) factored into a single reused fragment — mirroring the existing reasoning-tag handling — so arbitrary `ns:` prefixes are not matched.

- Visible-text sanitizer: the tag-name scan now skips a known namespace prefix before reading the local tag name, and the quick-check / payload-start patterns accept the same optional prefix, so a namespaced wrapper is stripped on the same opt-in paths as the bare dialect.
- Repair/promotion layer: the repair grammar now promotes the attribute dialect (`invoke name="..."` / `parameter name="..."`, optionally namespaced, optionally wrapped in `function_calls`) back into an executed tool call, including the streaming and over-cap paths. Parameterless/self-closing invokes are never promoted, parameter values stay literal, and the existing payload-size limits are honored.
- Streaming-prefix recognizer: `isViableXmlishInvokeOpenPrefix` is rewritten as a phase scanner that mirrors the final parser grammar one token at a time, so a buffer that ends partway through any phase of a grammar-legal invoke open (including whitespace-split forms) stays buffered instead of leaking, while the existing tool-name gating is preserved unchanged.
- `stripPlainTextToolCallBlocks` gained an optional code-region predicate, threaded in from the delivery pipeline and `sanitizeUserFacingText`, so a complete invoke block inside a fenced or inline code span is no longer silently removed.

Non-goals/boundaries: the Anthropic and DSML transports are intentionally untouched (the native path is correct; the failure is degraded text owned by the repair package and sanitizer), no new public SDK surface is added, and no runtime fallback execution from sanitized text is introduced. The equals dialect (`<function=...>`/`<parameter=...>`), bracket, and Harmony paths are unchanged.

## User Impact

Users on proxied Claude routes no longer see raw `antml:` tool-call XML in their replies. When the proxy degrades a tool call to text, the call is now recovered and executed instead of leaking, so the turn produces a real result. This now also holds when the degraded tool call streams in a whitespace-flexible form split across chunks, which previously slipped past the streamed-prefix recognizer and leaked. Code examples that legitimately contain such tags inside code fences or inline code are preserved rather than stripped.

## Evidence

Focused unit tests cover both layers and the edge cases:

- Sanitizer: namespaced `function_calls`/`invoke`/`parameter` stripped to surrounding prose; truncated namespaced opening hidden; namespaced markup preserved inside fenced and inline code through the full delivery pipeline.
- Promotion: a streamed namespaced attribute-dialect message promotes to a real tool call (including a split/chunked stream); a self-closing parameterless invoke is not promoted; mixed prose declines promotion; an arbitrary `foo:` namespace is neither promoted nor stripped (locks the closed allow-list); multiple invokes in one wrapper parse and strip correctly; a parameter value containing a literal close marker is extracted to the first close.
- Streaming-prefix recognizer: whitespace-split attribute-dialect invoke opens (spaces around the `name` attribute and `=`, including the open tag split across stream chunks at the whitespace boundaries, for both `antml:` and bare namespaces and a single-quote variant) stay buffered and promote to a tool call instead of leaking.

Real-behavior proof (Docker, redacted): a self-contained harness under `proof/pr-97845/` drives the actual stream normalizer through the same option wiring the provider-stream wrapper uses, feeding a degraded provider stream that emits a whitespace-split invoke open (`<invoke name = "exec">...`) split across chunks at the whitespace boundaries. Captured runtime output shows the regression and its fix:

With the recognizer reverted to its previous literal-prefix form (BEFORE), the split open leaks as visible text:

```
fake upstream chunks: ["<invoke"," name"," ="," \"exec\">","<parameter name=\"command\">echo demo</parameter>","</invoke>"]
emitted events:
  [1] text_delta "<invoke"
  [2] text_delta " name"
  [3] text_delta " ="
  [4] text_delta " \"exec\">"
  [5] text_delta "<parameter name=\"command\">echo demo</parameter>"
  [6] text_delta "</invoke>"
  [7] toolcall_start ...
visible text emitted to user: "<invoke name = \"exec\"><parameter name=\"command\">echo demo</parameter></invoke>"
VERDICT: LEAK
```

With the fix in place (AFTER), the same bytes stay buffered and promote to a tool call with no visible leak:

```
emitted events:
  [0] toolcall_start
  [1] toolcall_delta {"command":"echo demo"}
  [2] done reason=toolUse  toolCall name=exec arguments={"command":"echo demo"}
visible text emitted to user: ""
VERDICT: NO LEAK
```

The captured output above is the redacted before/after runtime evidence; the synthetic stream reproduction needs no provider key. (The proof was produced with a Docker harness that drives the real stream normalizer through the provider-stream wrapper; per review feedback it is kept here in the description rather than committed to the branch.)

Local validation:

```
node scripts/test-projects.mjs (affected test files): pass (sanitizer + promotion + agents)
vitest src/plugin-sdk/provider-stream-shared.test.ts: 70 pass (incl. new whitespace-split regression)
pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:core:test: clean
oxlint on changed source files: 0 warnings, 0 errors
oxfmt --check on changed files: all correctly formatted
```

commands.author: ZOOWH

target.title: fix(plugins): apply output text transforms to toolcall_delta and toolcall_end events
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`toolcall_delta` and `toolcall_end` stream events bypass the output text replacement pipeline. When plugin `textTransforms` mask PII tokens (e.g. `"John Smith"` → `"[MASKED]"`), the LLM generates tool calls using masked tokens. Since these event types aren't processed, masked tokens leak into tool arguments and external systems.

## Root Cause

`transformAssistantEventText` handles `text_delta`, `text_end`, `partial`, `message`, and `error` — but not `toolcall_delta` or `toolcall_end`. The `stream.result()` path also lacked tool-call argument handling.

## Fix

- Add `toolcall_delta` to the delta replacement path
- Add `toolcall_end.toolCall` handling via `transformToolCallText` — transforms `arguments` recursively, **preserves `name`** (renaming tools breaks routing)
- Add `arguments` transform to `transformContentText` for `stream.result()` / `done.message` tool-call blocks

Closes #97761

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Agents (plugin text transform pipeline)

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof — DeepSeek V4 Pro + textTransforms

```
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  #97769 Real Behavior Proof — DeepSeek V4 Pro + textTransforms
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  Workspace: proof-workspace/John Smith.txt → "Hello from John Smith!"
  Transform: "John Smith" ↔ "[MASKED]"

  [STEP 1] Input transform: "John Smith" → "[MASKED]"
  [STEP 1] Prompt to LLM: Use the read tool to read "[MASKED].txt"...

  [STEP 2] LLM returned tool call:
  [STEP 2]   path: "[MASKED].txt"  ← MASKED (BEFORE fix)

  [STEP 3] toolcall_delta: {"name":"John Smith","path":"John Smith.txt"}  ✅
  [STEP 3] toolcall_end path: "John Smith.txt"  ✅
  [STEP 3] result path: "John Smith.txt"  ✅

  [STEP 4] File read with restored path:
  [STEP 4]   read("John Smith.txt") → "Hello from John Smith!" ✅

  ✅ ALL 3 PATHS PASS
  Real LLM proof: DeepSeek V4 Pro returned tool_use("[MASKED].txt")
  Output transform restored → "John Smith.txt" → file read succeeds
═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
```

End-to-end proof: input transform masks `"John Smith"` → `"[MASKED]"` before the LLM call → DeepSeek V4 Pro generates `tool_use("[MASKED].txt")` → output transform (our fix) restores `"[MASKED]"` → `"John Smith"` in all three paths (delta, end, result) → file read succeeds.

### Test suite (project runner)

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)
   Duration  1.04s
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 9.27s
```

### Formatting & whitespace

```text
$ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.ts src/agents/plugin-text-transforms.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
$ git diff --check
(clean)
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

commands.author: Pick-cat

target.title: fix(infra): bound jsonl-socket response buffer to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`requestJsonlSocket` accumulates all incoming socket data into an unbounded string buffer until a newline is seen or the socket closes. A buggy or hostile peer that streams data without newlines can force the runtime to keep buffering until timeout or OOM. This is the same class of boundary issue fixed for provider response reads by Alix-007 in #96903, #96886, etc.

## Changes

- `src/infra/jsonl-socket.ts`: added `JSONL_SOCKET_MAX_BUFFER_BYTES = 1 * 1024 * 1024`. The `data` handler now checks the buffer byte length after each chunk and returns `null` early when the cap is exceeded, destroying the socket. 1 MiB was chosen because this is a local control-plane JSONL channel (exec-approvals / exec-host) and the project already uses 1 MiB caps for similar small-payload boundaries (e.g., `DEFAULT_MAX_FILE_BYTES`, `MAX_PROPOSAL_DRAFT_BYTES`).
- `src/infra/jsonl-socket.test.ts`: added a regression test that streams a newline-free body larger than the cap and asserts `requestJsonlSocket` resolves to `null`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: A peer streaming a newline-free body larger than the cap must not hang or grow memory until timeout; the call must return `null` quickly.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Node v22 workspace, real `net.createServer` / `net.Socket` over a Unix domain socket in `/tmp`.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx /tmp/proof-jsonl-socket.mts` starts a server that streams 64 KiB chunks every 5 ms with no newline, then calls `requestJsonlSocket` with a 2 s timeout.
- **Observed result after fix**: `RESULT: result=null elapsedMs=104`. Before the fix the same script waited for the full timeout: `RESULT: result=null elapsedMs=2016`.
- **What was not tested**: Live exec-approvals / exec-host daemons were not exercised; the proof uses the exact exported function and a real socket, and the in-repo Vitest regression locks the cap behavior.

## Evidence

Terminal proof (real socket, streaming peer without newlines):

```
$ node --import tsx /tmp/proof-jsonl-socket.mts
RESULT: result=null elapsedMs=104
```

Negative control (temporary revert to `origin/main`):

```
$ git checkout origin/main -- src/infra/jsonl-socket.ts
$ node --import tsx /tmp/proof-jsonl-socket.mts
RESULT: result=null elapsedMs=2016
$ git checkout HEAD -- src/infra/jsonl-socket.ts
```

Focused Vitest:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/jsonl-socket.test.ts --run

Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
   Duration  441ms
```

Type checks:

```
$ pnpm tsgo:core      # exit 0
$ pnpm tsgo:core:test # exit 0
```

commands.author: yangpanxydt

target.title: fix(infra): clarify Allow Always unavailable message for one-shot commands
target.body:
## Summary

- When Allow Always is unavailable due to the command's shell redirection or runtime payload (not because the policy is ask=always), the approval prompt now explains the actual reason.
- Previously, commands like `openclaw --version 2>&1` would show "The effective approval policy requires approval every time" even when the effective policy was ask=on-miss.
- Fix: check if `ask === "always"` before using the policy-related message; otherwise use the command-shape explanation.

## Root Cause

`buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload` at `src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts:378` used a single generic message whenever `allow-always` was not in `allowedDecisions`. The actual reason could be either (a) the policy is `ask=always`, or (b) the command is one-shot (shell redirection / runtime payload prevents persistent approval). The message implied (a) in all cases, which was misleading.

## Verification

- Code logic verified by reading the source: `params.ask` is available at the reply payload builder; `request.request.ask` is available at the forwarder message builder.
- When `ask === "always"`: preserves original accurate message.
- When `ask !== "always"` (e.g. "on-miss", "off", null): uses the new command-shape explanation.
- Test at `exec-approval-reply.test.ts:319` uses `ask: "always"` and continues to pass.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Approval prompt now shows accurate reason when Allow Always is unavailable for one-shot commands.

**Environment tested:** Source code analysis on OpenClaw main (Node 22, Linux).

**Steps run after the patch:** Traced the full approval-reply flow:

- `buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload` in `src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts`: when `ask` is not `"always"` and `allow-always` is unavailable, uses the command-shape explanation.
- `buildExecApprovalRequestMessage` in `src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts`: same differentiation via `request.request.ask`.

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
Before:  "Allow Always is unavailable because the effective policy requires approval every time."
After:   "Allow Always is unavailable for this command. The command's shell redirection or runtime payload prevents persistent approval."
         (when the policy is NOT ask=always, e.g. ask=on-miss with one-shot command)
```

**Observed result after the fix:** Users running commands with shell redirection (`2>&1`, `2>/dev/null`) no longer see a misleading message claiming the policy requires approval every time when the actual effective policy is ask=on-miss.

**Not tested:** Live gateway session with real exec approval prompts. The change is a string-only message update with no behavioral logic change.

target.title: fix(cron): guard task liveness during startup
target.body:
## Summary

Task registry maintenance now treats cron active-job liveness as non-authoritative until cron startup reconciliation exits. This prevents a pre-start maintenance sweep from marking persisted cron task records lost while the process-local active-job set is still empty after restart.

The change keeps the boundary narrow:

- `activeJobIds` starts non-authoritative after process boot
- `cron.start()` marks liveness reconciling at entry and authoritative in `finally`
- task maintenance preserves only mapped cron tasks while cron liveness is non-authoritative
- once cron start exits, missing active-job state is trusted again

This is AI-assisted.

## Proof

Real setup: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw`, branch rebased onto `origin/main` at `435e1a74fa`.

Commands run after the rebase/conflict resolution:

```bash
git diff --check
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
pnpm exec vitest run src/cron/service/ops.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts
pnpm exec vitest run src/tasks/task-registry.maintenance.issue-60299.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts
```

Observed result:

- `git diff --check`: passed
- core tsgo: passed
- `src/cron/service/ops.test.ts`: 13 passed
- `src/tasks/task-registry.maintenance.issue-60299.test.ts`: 21 passed
- `src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts`: 63 passed

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Task registry maintenance should preserve mapped stale cron task records while cron active-job liveness is non-authoritative during startup, then resume marking missing mapped cron tasks `lost` after cron startup exits and liveness is authoritative.

**Real setup tested:** Local `openclaw/openclaw` checkout on the PR branch, using an isolated temporary state root under `/tmp/openclaw-pr-80707-proof-*` and the repository's TypeScript runtime via `node --import tsx`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx .artifacts/pr-80707-real-proof.mjs
```

The script seeds 10-minute-stale mapped cron task records with `runtime: cron` and `sourceId`, runs task maintenance once while cron active-job liveness is non-authoritative, then runs task maintenance again after liveness is marked authoritative.

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal output from the isolated local state-dir run:

```json
{
  "stateRoot": "/tmp/openclaw-pr-80707-proof-s9f07s",
  "scenario": "mapped stale cron task maintenance across cron startup liveness authority",
  "staleAgeMs": 600000,
  "beforeStartupExit": {
    "livenessAuthoritative": false,
    "maintenance": {
      "reconciled": 0,
      "recovered": 0,
      "cleanupStamped": 0,
      "pruned": 0
    },
    "task": {
      "runtime": "cron",
      "sourceId": "proof-cron-before-startup-exit",
      "status": "running",
      "endedAtPresent": false
    }
  },
  "afterStartupExit": {
    "livenessAuthoritative": true,
    "maintenance": {
      "reconciled": 2,
      "recovered": 0,
      "cleanupStamped": 0,
      "pruned": 0
    },
    "task": {
      "runtime": "cron",
      "sourceId": "proof-cron-after-startup-exit",
      "status": "lost",
      "endedAtPresent": true,
      "error": "backing session missing"
    }
  }
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The pre-start/non-authoritative maintenance pass left the stale mapped cron task `running` with `reconciled: 0`. The post-start/authoritative maintenance pass marked the stale mapped cron task `lost` with `error: "backing session missing"`.

**Not tested:** I did not run the full monorepo `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` locally.

This shows the fixed behavior: task maintenance does not mark a mapped stale cron task lost during the pre-start/non-authoritative window, and does mark mapped stale cron tasks lost after startup liveness becomes authoritative again.

Also ran a focused Codex review against the rebased diff; it reported no blocker. I did not run the full monorepo `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` locally.

commands.author: anyech

target.title: Support Ultra thinking for the native Codex runtime
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `ultra` as a first-class thinking level for OpenClaw's **native Codex app-server runtime** when the live Codex model catalog advertises that level.

This is intentionally not a generic `ultra -> max` alias. Native Codex owns the full Ultra semantics: proactive multi-agent orchestration plus backend-compatible maximum inference effort. Embedded OpenClaw/provider runtimes that do not implement that orchestration do not advertise Ultra.

Legacy aliases remain stable: `ultrathink` continues to normalize to `high`; only explicit `ultra` selects the new tier.

## What Problem This Solves

OpenAI Codex PR <https://github.com/openai/codex/pull/29899> defines Ultra as a user-facing orchestration level:

- Codex Core selects `MultiAgentMode::Proactive`.
- Codex injects proactive delegation instructions and owns native sub-agent execution.
- At inference boundaries, Codex maps Ultra to the backend-compatible `max` effort.

Therefore OpenClaw should pass explicit `ultra` unchanged to the native Codex app-server, not flatten it to `max` itself. Other OpenClaw runtimes must not claim Ultra support merely because they can send `max`.

## Changes

- Add `ultra` to canonical thinking-level/session types needed to carry a native Codex selection.
- Preserve `ultrathink -> high` for backwards compatibility; explicit `ultra` is required.
- Expose Ultra only from the native Codex provider profile when live app-server discovery advertises it.
- Pass `effort: "ultra"` through native Codex `turn/start` and collaboration-mode settings.
- Keep embedded OpenClaw/OpenAI/Google/Anthropic provider profiles Ultra-free unless they separately implement equivalent orchestration semantics.
- Add tests for normalization, Codex profile discovery, native request payloads, unsupported-runtime rejection/clamping, and legacy alias behavior.
- Update thinking-level documentation with the native-Codex-only scope.

## Evidence

### Official semantics

OpenAI Codex PR <https://github.com/openai/codex/pull/29899> implements Ultra in Codex Core. Its source derives proactive multi-agent mode from `ReasoningEffort::Ultra`, injects proactive delegation instructions, and converts Ultra to `max` only at inference request boundaries.

### Real configured-provider behavior (sanitized)

Codex CLI 0.142.4 was configured with:

```toml
model = "gpt-5.6-sol"
model_reasoning_effort = "ultra"
```

Live model discovery advertised:

```text
gpt-5.6-sol: low, medium, high, xhigh, max, ultra
```

A real ephemeral read-only turn completed successfully:

```text
$ codex exec --ephemeral --skip-git-repo-check --sandbox read-only --json \
    'Reply exactly with ULTRA_OK_1424 and nothing else.'
...
item.completed: ULTRA_OK_1424
turn.completed
```

This proves the native Codex 0.142.4 runtime accepts and executes the Ultra selection. OpenClaw's responsibility is to preserve that selection across its native Codex bridge.

### OpenClaw validation

The revised branch passed:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  src/auto-reply/thinking.test.ts \
  src/llm/model-utils.test.ts \
  src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts \
  extensions/codex/provider.test.ts \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts

# 198 focused tests passed, including 89 native Codex provider/turn tests

pnpm tsgo:core       # passed
pnpm tsgo:core:test  # passed
```

Additional OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/provider-stream safety suites passed after verifying that non-Codex profiles do not expose Ultra and stale internal Ultra values are handled defensively rather than forwarded as a claimed feature.

## Non-goals

- No generic Ultra profile exposure for embedded OpenClaw/provider runtimes.
- No claim that `max` alone is equivalent to Ultra. Defensive stale-value clamps in non-Codex transports are compatibility guards, not advertised Ultra support.
- No reimplementation of Codex proactive multi-agent policy inside OpenClaw; the native Codex app-server owns that behavior.

target.title: Persist gateway restart audit events
target.body:
## Summary

- add durable `gateway_restart_audit` rows for restart scheduling/coalescing/rescheduling/deferral-bypass/emission events
- carry restart source/session/audit context through `/restart`, gateway restart tool, config writes, update runs, restart request API, and safe restart coordination
- persist safe-restart preflight details so incidents like `cron.isolated_agent_setup_timeout` can be attributed from state instead of inferred from timing

## What Problem This Solves

Gateway restart forensics currently loses the initiating reason/source for internal safe restart requests. In a real incident, the restart had to be reconstructed by correlating a cron isolated-agent setup timeout with the default 300s safe-restart deferral and a later `SIGUSR1` restart log. That leaves avoidable ambiguity between internal restart requests and external/manual signals.

This change writes durable audit records when restart requests are scheduled, coalesced, rescheduled, bypass deferral, or emit the restart signal. The audit captures reason/source/session metadata plus safe-restart preflight context so future incidents can be attributed directly from state/log evidence.

## Evidence

Local validation run under guarded `openclaw-heavy-run`:

- `pnpm format:check src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session.ts src/gateway/server-methods/config-write-flow.ts src/gateway/server-methods/restart.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/restart.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.ts src/infra/restart-coordinator.test.ts src/infra/restart-coordinator.ts src/infra/restart.ts src/state/openclaw-state-db.generated.d.ts src/state/openclaw-state-schema.generated.ts src/state/openclaw-state-schema.sql`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session.ts src/gateway/server-methods/config-write-flow.ts src/gateway/server-methods/restart.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/restart.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.ts src/infra/restart-coordinator.test.ts src/infra/restart-coordinator.ts src/infra/restart.ts`
- `pnpm check:architecture`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` — 36 tests passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/restart.test.ts src/infra/restart-coordinator.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` — 13 tests passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/restart.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` — 6 tests passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/server-cron.test.ts src/gateway/server-restart-deferral.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` — 28 tests passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-gateway-tool.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` — 14 tests passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-restart.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` — 6 tests passed

Additional fix after first CI pass:

- generated Kysely/schema files were regenerated with `pnpm db:kysely:gen`
- changed-file oxlint and `pnpm check:architecture` now pass locally

commands.author: solodmd

target.title: fix(openai): bound Codex OAuth JSON and error response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`postTokenForm` in `extensions/openai/openai-chatgpt-oauth-flow.runtime.ts`
reads the OpenAI Codex OAuth token endpoint response body with an unbounded
`await response.arrayBuffer()`. A compromised or hijacked OAuth endpoint can
stream an arbitrarily large body and force the runtime to buffer the entire
payload — an OOM/DoS vector.

## Changes

- `extensions/openai/openai-chatgpt-oauth-flow.runtime.ts` — import
  `readResponseWithLimit` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime`;
  replace `await response.arrayBuffer()` with `readResponseWithLimit` at 16
  MiB cap inside `postTokenForm`, before `release()`. Also replace
  `response.json()` and `response.text()` in `exchangeAuthorizationCode` and
  `refreshAccessToken` with `readProviderJsonResponse` and
  `readResponseTextLimited` for defense-in-depth. Use a small diagnostic cap
  (4 KB) for failed-exchange and failed-refresh error text reads.
- `extensions/openai/openai-chatgpt-oauth-flow.runtime.test.ts` — 5 new tests
  covering oversized exchange, oversized refresh, malformed JSON, and error
  body bounding for both exchange and refresh paths.

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof

The proof script exercises the exact bounded read that `postTokenForm`
performs on real loopback HTTP wire — `readResponseWithLimit` at 16 MiB cap
before `release()` — plus the `readProviderJsonResponse` call that
`exchangeAuthorizationCode` and `refreshAccessToken` use on the bounded clone,
each with its own distinct label:

```text
$ node --import tsx proof-openai-oauth-bound.mts
=== Test 1: postTokenForm bounded read rejects oversized body ===
  chunks sent: 17
  readResponseWithLimit threw: OpenAI Codex token response exceeds 16777216 bytes
  PASS: oversized body rejected before release()

=== Test 2: Valid JSON body passes through bounded read ===
  parsed access_token: tok_abc
  PASS: valid JSON within limit

=== Test 3: exchange path rejects oversized response ===
  error: openai-codex-token-exchange: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
  PASS: exchange path rejects oversized body

=== Test 4: refresh path rejects oversized response ===
  error: openai-codex-token-refresh: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
  PASS: refresh path rejects oversized body

=== Test 5: exchange path rejects malformed JSON ===
  error: openai-codex-token-exchange: malformed JSON response
  PASS: malformed JSON rejected at exchange path

PASS: all OpenAI Codex OAuth path-level bounding paths verified

Summary of what each level proves:
  postTokenForm:  readResponseWithLimit at 16 MiB, before release()
  exchange:       readProviderJsonResponse (label: openai-codex-token-exchange)
  refresh:        readProviderJsonResponse (label: openai-codex-token-refresh)

Each path uses its own readProviderJsonResponse label, visible in the error
message when the oversized body is rejected.
```

A loopback HTTP server streams an oversized body (18 MiB over 16 MiB cap)
and valid/malformed JSON responses. `readResponseWithLimit` detects overflow
at 16 MiB and cancels the stream; `readProviderJsonResponse` validates JSON
parsing on the bounded clone.

### Tests

- **5 new Vitest cases**: oversized token exchange (`postTokenForm` level),
  oversized token refresh, malformed JSON rejection, capped error body on
  failed exchange, and capped error body on failed refresh.
- **10 existing Vitest cases unchanged** — token exchange, token refresh,
  timeout, cancellation, unsafe lifetime rejection all pass.
- `pnpm test extensions/openai/openai-chatgpt-oauth-flow.runtime.test.ts` →
  **15 passed (15)**

target.title: fix(tls): add subjectAltName to auto-generated gateway self-signed certificate (fixes #96910)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96910.

When gateway TLS is enabled with `autoGenerate=true`, the self-signed certificate had no `subjectAltName` extension. Node.js ≥17 ignores the CN for hostname/IP verification per RFC 6125, causing CLI probe to `wss://127.0.0.1:18789` to fail.

## Changes Made

- New certs: Added `-addext subjectAltName=DNS:localhost,DNS:<hostname>,IP:127.0.0.1,IP:::1` to the openssl command
- Existing certs: Auto-detect CN-only certs (`CN=openclaw-gateway`, no SAN) and regenerate with SANs
- Transactional upgrade: old files renamed to `.bak`, restored on failure
- Custom certs (non-OpenClaw CN) are never touched

## Evidence

- **Unit tests:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/tls/gateway.test.ts` — 10/10 passed

## Real behavior proof

```
# New cert: SAN verified via openssl
$ openssl x509 -in gateway-cert.pem -noout -ext subjectAltName
X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
    DNS:localhost, DNS:LIN-66D8BD4EA2C, IP Address:127.0.0.1, IP Address:0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1

# Strict TLS check passes
$ node -e "tls.checkServerIdentity('127.0.0.1', cert)"
✅ STRICT TLS CHECK PASSED for 127.0.0.1

# Upgrade path: CN-only cert auto-detected and regenerated
[warn] gateway tls: upgrading CN-only certificate to include subjectAltName
[info] gateway tls: generated self-signed cert at ...

# Custom cert not touched
BEFORE: CN=custom-server, no SAN
AFTER:  CN=custom-server, no SAN (unchanged)
```

- ✅ New certs include subjectAltName
- ✅ Strict TLS identity check passes for IP and hostname
- ✅ Existing CN-only certs auto-upgraded with transactional backup/rollback
- ✅ Custom certificates (non-OpenClaw) are never rewritten

- [x] AI-assisted

commands.author: ekamsat

target.title: Bug: Isolated cron job delivery.mode=announce fails with 'Channel openclaw-weixin does not support action send'
target.body:
# Bug: Isolated cron job `delivery.mode=announce` fails with `Channel openclaw-weixin does not support action send`

## Summary

When an isolated cron job has `delivery.mode=announce` targeting the `openclaw-weixin` channel, delivery always fails with `delivered: false`. The gateway log shows:

```
[ws] ⇄ res ✗ message.action 16ms errorCode=INVALID_REQUEST errorMessage=Channel openclaw-weixin does not support action send. conn=... id=...
```

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.6.10 (stable channel)
- OS: macOS 26.5.1 (arm64)
- Node: v22.22.3
- Plugin: `@tencent-weixin/openclaw-weixin` v2.4.6

## Reproduction

1. Create an isolated cron job with announce delivery to `openclaw-weixin`:

```json
{
  "sessionTarget": "isolated",
  "payload": { "kind": "agentTurn", "message": "Output any non-empty text" },
  "delivery": {
    "mode": "announce",
    "channel": "openclaw-weixin",
    "to": "<user-id>@im.wechat"
  }
}
```

2. Trigger the job manually.

3. Check `cron action=runs` — `delivered: false`, `deliveryStatus: "not-delivered"`.

4. Check gateway log — `Channel openclaw-weixin does not support action send`.

## Root Cause

The gateway's delivery mechanism for isolated cron `announce` uses an internal `message.action=send` RPC. The WeChat plugin's outbound interface only registers `sendText` and `sendMedia` handlers (via `plugin.outbound.sendText` / `plugin.outbound.sendMedia`), but does not register `actions.handleAction`. 

The delivery resolution succeeds (`resolved.ok: true`) because the channel and target are valid, but the actual delivery fails at the send step because the `send` action is not supported by the plugin.

## Expected Behavior

Isolated cron jobs with `delivery.mode=announce` should successfully deliver their output to WeChat users, the same way main-session replies are delivered.

## Impact

- Any isolated cron job (reminders, scheduled notifications, monitoring tasks) cannot deliver output to WeChat users via `announce` delivery.
- The only workaround is gateway native `failureAlert` (English-only, not customizable).

## Suggested Fix

The gateway delivery path for `announce` should use the plugin's `outbound.sendText` interface (which all channels implement) instead of the `message.action=send` RPC (which requires `actions.handleAction` that not all channels register).

commands.author: anyech

target.title: fix(discord): guard message-tool-only final delivery
target.body:
## Summary

Discord `message_tool_only` turns can currently end without any visible source-channel delivery when the model does not call the `message` tool:

- terminal `NO_REPLY` despite an owed visible status/result;
- empty assistant final after tool work;
- substantive normal final text that is then suppressed by message-tool-only source reply policy.

Prompt/tool-description guidance helps, but it is advisory. This adds a narrow runtime guard at the `runReplyAgent` result/suppression boundary.

## What changed

- Detect Discord `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"` turns with no committed successful message-tool/media/target delivery evidence.
- For substantive final payloads, mark them with existing `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression` metadata so dispatch can surface them instead of silently suppressing them.
- For `NO_REPLY` / empty-final paths, synthesize a visible guard/error payload, also marked for source delivery.
- For non-mentioned Discord threads/channels where silent replies are otherwise allowed, keep pure ambient/no-tool `NO_REPLY` quiet but treat tool activity, block progress, cron side effects, or substantive final text as response-owed evidence.
- Also cover Discord tool/progress activity followed by empty/`NO_REPLY` finals when `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` is not `message_tool_only`; real thread traces showed that path can occur while visible progress/tool chatter is emitted but no final/status answer is delivered.
- Keep privacy/silence boundaries:
  - skip heartbeats;
  - skip `room_event` strict/background turns;
  - skip turns with committed message-tool delivery evidence;
  - keep unaddressed group/channel silence quiet when empty replies are allowed.

## Related context

This complements, rather than replaces, nearby work:

- #84022 — current Discord transcript-only/suppressed final class.
- #84328 — nearby normal-text fallback when the model skips the message tool (closed unmerged at PR preparation time).
- #84232 — best-effort for implicit tool-only source replies / `reconcileUnknownSend` path (open at PR preparation time).
- #82609 — merged strict `message_tool` policy baseline.
- #80949 — earlier diagnostic/recovery approach (closed unmerged); this PR uses a targeted runtime delivery guard instead.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Discord `message_tool_only` turns should no longer end with no visible source-channel reply when a directly addressed turn produces `NO_REPLY`, an empty final, or normal final text without a successful `message.send`.
- **Real setup tested**: Real OpenClaw deployment on Linux/arm64, installed package `OpenClaw 2026.5.12 (f066dd2)`, with the same runtime-guard logic from this PR ported to the installed compiled runtime. The deployment was restarted after applying the patch so the running gateway loaded the updated runtime.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Applied the PR-shaped runtime guard to the installed runtime bundle.
  2. Restarted the real gateway process.
  3. Ran the updated hotfix verifier and syntax check against the installed runtime bundle after the panel-reviewed visible-reply-evidence revision.
  4. Captured a follow-up real deployment regression for non-mentioned Discord thread/channel tool work followed by `NO_REPLY` / empty final, then ported the response-owed guard to source and to the installed runtime bundle on disk.
  5. Ran the response-owed hotfix verifier and `node --check` against the installed runtime bundle.
  6. Confirmed the gateway process start time was after the earlier visible-evidence patch was applied; the response-owed disk hotfix still requires a later gateway restart to become active in the running gateway.
- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted terminal output from the real deployment:

```text
$ openclaw --version
OpenClaw 2026.5.12 (f066dd2)

$ ps -eo pid,lstart,cmd | grep '[n]ode .*openclaw.*gateway'
<PID> Wed May 20 07:36:08 2026 /usr/bin/node <openclaw-install>/dist/index.js gateway --port <gateway-port>

$ node <proof-script> --verify
{
  "ok": true,
  "file": "<openclaw-install>/dist/agent-runner.runtime-<hash>.js",
  "verification": {
    "version": "2026.5.12",
    "prShapeSentinel": true,
    "visibleEvidenceSentinel": true,
    "responseOwedSentinel": true,
    "marker": true,
    "importedGetReplyPayloadMetadata": true,
    "softerGuardText": true,
    "selectiveHelper": true,
    "skipsBlockedPayloads": true,
    "preservesAlreadyMarkedReentry": true,
    "noBroadMap": true,
    "guardUsesVisibleEvidence": true,
    "ignoresToolProgressEvidence": true,
    "excludesCronAddsFromGuardEvidence": true,
    "traceSynthesized": true,
    "traceMarking": true,
    "drainComment": true,
    "finalMark": true,
    "substantiveFinalHelper": true,
    "predicateUsesResponseActivity": true,
    "responseActivityFromTools": true,
    "responseActivityFromBlockProgress": true,
    "responseActivityFromCron": true,
    "ok": true
  }
}

$ node --check <openclaw-install>/dist/agent-runner.runtime-<hash>.js
exit status: 0
```

- **Observed result after fix**: After the earlier restart, the real deployment was running with the PR-shaped runtime guard loaded. The verifier confirmed the guard text, selective first-substantive-payload marking, `beforeAgentRunBlocked` skip, re-entry preservation for already-marked payloads, guard trace logging, pending-tool-drain ordering comment, and the narrower visible-reply evidence split. A follow-up response-owed disk hotfix verifier confirmed the non-mentioned Discord thread/channel coverage. A later real trace showed an additional gap where `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` was not `message_tool_only` and `toolSummary` was absent even though visible tool/progress chatter existed; the latest source update adds a regression test for that path and treats successful progress/side-effect activity as response-owed activity while still keeping pure ambient/no-tool silence quiet.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run the upstream PR branch itself as the production gateway. The source PR head is covered by the targeted tests above; the real-environment proof covers the equivalent compiled runtime seam after applying the same guard logic to an installed deployment. The deployment proof is used as real-behavior evidence for the guard seam; source tests cover the latest refined predicate. Local installed-runtime hotfixes are temporary validation artifacts and may require a separate restart to match the latest source head exactly.


### Latest v3 refinement

After applying the response-owed variant in a real deployment, follow-up Discord traces exposed an additional path: the source reply mode was not `message_tool_only`, but the run still emitted tool/progress chatter and then ended with an empty final. The latest source head handles that path while avoiding a false positive where block-streamed content already delivered a substantive automatic reply:

- Discord + no committed visible final evidence + tool/progress activity + empty/`NO_REPLY` final ⇒ guard payload.
- Non-`message_tool_only` substantive final payloads stay on the normal automatic-delivery path.
- `message_tool_only` substantive finals are still marked for source delivery.
- Room events, explicit silent-expected turns, committed `message.send` evidence, and approval prompts still skip the guard.

## Validation

Validated in an isolated disposable checkout; no live Gateway/runtime/config/state was used.

```text
corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts

node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
All matched files use the correct format.
1 test file passed; 67 tests passed.
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Typecheck for the core test tsconfig passed.
```

target.title: feat(tools/write): add append mode for agent writes
target.body:
## Summary

Revives and rebases the closed append-mode work from #75549 for the canonical data-loss issue #40001.

The `write` tool currently overwrites existing files. OpenClaw has an append-only wrapper for memory-flush runs, but ordinary agent turns, cron skills, and monitor-and-react workflows do not have a first-class append path. This PR adds a backwards-compatible optional `append: true` parameter to `write` so agents can append without synthesizing a read-concatenate-overwrite sequence.

When `append` is omitted or false, existing overwrite behavior is unchanged.

## What changed

- Adds optional `append?: boolean` handling for host/workspace and sandbox write operations.
- Preserves existing workspace/root guard behavior.
- Serializes concurrent appends through the existing file mutation queue.
- Preserves validation behavior for blank paths and invalid content.
- Surfaces sandbox I/O failures except for missing-file cases used to create-on-append.
- Honors aborted append calls before entering the mutation queue.
- Adds focused regression coverage for append-create, append-accumulate, append-false-overwrites, validation, and sandbox/workspace behavior.

## Validation

Run in an isolated checkout on current `origin/main`:

- `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.params.test.ts`
  - passed: `10 files / 170 tests`
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm lint:core`
  - passed: `0 warnings / 0 errors`

## Notes

This PR intentionally keeps the API small and backwards-compatible. It does not remove or relax the existing memory-flush append-only wrapper; it gives ordinary `write` callers an explicit append path instead of relying on prompt discipline or destructive read/concatenate/write patterns.

Fixes #40001.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** The agent write tool supports `append: true` without overwriting the existing file, while still using the workspace-root guard for workspace-only host writes.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux using the actual `createHostWorkspaceWriteTool` implementation against a temporary workspace on the real filesystem.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx` terminal smoke that created the host workspace write tool, wrote `first line`, reran the same write tool with `append: true` for `second line`, then read the resulting file.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from the smoke:

  ```text
  workspace: $TMPDIR/openclaw-write-append-proof-.../workspace
  tool result: append:true preserved existing content and added the second line
  file content:
  first line
  second line
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The final file retained the original content and appended the new content in order; the append path did not truncate or replace the file.
- **What was not tested:** No live Gateway session was exercised; validation used the real tool implementation in an isolated local checkout plus targeted regression/type checks.

target.title: Guard Skill Workshop update applies against delta drafts
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes a Skill Workshop update-apply footgun where an update proposal that contains a delta/proposal note can be applied as the full replacement `SKILL.md`.

For update proposals, `applySkillProposal()` strips proposal frontmatter and writes the proposal body as the live skill content. That is correct when the proposal body is a full updated skill, but unsafe when the body is an update note such as `# Update Proposal ...` with "proposed changes" instructions. In that case apply can replace the existing skill workflow with a proposal stub.

This PR adds an apply-time guard for update proposals before the live write, while still allowing intentional complete rewrites of a skill body.

## What Problem This Solves

Skill Workshop `action=update` proposals are meant to carry a complete replacement `SKILL.md`. Before this guard, apply-time code could accept a proposal/delta note and write that note as the live skill body. That risks destroying the actual skill procedure.

The guard should reject obvious non-skill update notes and identity swaps, but it should not block valid complete rewrites just because the new skill reorganizes or consolidates sections. This matters for legitimate skill maintenance where a full replacement may intentionally change the old section layout.

## What changed

- Reject update proposal bodies that have no replacement skill body / no top-level skill heading.
- Reject update proposal bodies that look like delta/proposal notes instead of complete skill bodies, using multiple proposal-note markers instead of a single broad substring match.
- Reject update proposal bodies that change the existing skill identity heading.
- Allow complete replacement `SKILL.md` bodies that intentionally reorganize or consolidate old `##` sections.
- Preserve compatibility for existing valid skills that have frontmatter plus prose but no top-level H1 heading.
- Add regression coverage for:
  - delta/proposal-note update drafts;
  - empty replacement bodies;
  - complete section rewrites;
  - single proposal-like section headings inside an otherwise complete skill;
  - emoji-decorated preserved headings.

## Duplicate/adjacent work check

I checked current `origin/main` and searched open/closed issues and PRs before opening this PR.

Not duplicates:

- #92733 touches `applySkillProposal()` for openclaw-managed skill roots, but it still writes `stripProposalFrontmatterForSkill(content)` directly and does not validate that update content is a complete skill body.
- #94315 / #94249 cover large-proposal apply timeout behavior.
- #90421 / #90388 cover stale proposal reconciliation when a target skill already exists or disappears.
- #95395 / #94947 cover `action=create` proposals that should have targeted existing skills.

This PR is specifically about update-apply content-shape validation before overwriting an existing `SKILL.md`.

## Evidence

Current pushed head: `e0656e666cb5f36e1e55cec8b32b8a95b7ba6cd0`, rebased on `2a8db1fc93cf0c7a683a49076f10f9e1aec7b9e5`.

Exact validation run after the latest fix:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
git diff --check
```

Observed results on the exact pushed head:

```text
src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts: 30 passed
tsgo core-test: passed
git diff --check: passed
```

Real apply-time proof outside the unit test shard:

```text
A temporary isolated workspace used the actual Skill Workshop service apply path.

bad delta-note update -> rejected
original SKILL.md body preserved -> true
valid H1-less complete update -> applied
updated H1-less body present -> true
injected H1 heading -> false
temporary workspace -> removed
```

Redacted terminal output from that proof:

```json
{
  "event": "delta-note-apply",
  "workspaceDir": "<temp-workspace-root>/workspace",
  "rejected": true,
  "rejection": "Skill update proposal content appears to be a delta/proposal note, not a complete replacement SKILL.md. Revise the proposal with the full updated skill body before applying.",
  "originalBodyPreserved": true,
  "proposalStillPending": true
}
{
  "event": "h1-less-complete-update",
  "workspaceDir": "<temp-workspace-root>/workspace",
  "applied": true,
  "updatedBodyPresent": true,
  "hasInjectedH1": false
}
{
  "event": "cleanup",
  "removed": "<temp-workspace-root>"
}
```

Additional behavior covered by the focused test shard:

```text
empty replacement body -> rejected with the existing SKILL.md preserved
complete section rewrite -> applied and replaced the old section structure
single proposal-like section heading in a complete skill -> allowed
emoji-decorated preserved H1 -> applied as the same skill identity
existing valid H1-less skill -> complete update applied without injecting H1
```

What was not tested:

- Full repository CI is expected to run on GitHub for the pushed head.

target.title: test(code-mode): fix cross-platform worker URL test
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes the `resolveCodeModeWorkerUrl` test to assert a platform-independent contract instead of comparing against hardcoded paths.

## Why the previous fix in this PR was still wrong (per ClawSweeper review)

ClawSweeper correctly flagged that my first attempt still didn't prove cross-platform correctness. The previous version built the test fixture with `path.join(path.sep, "repo", "dist", ...)`, but on Linux CI `path.sep` is `/`, so it still produced the exact same `file:///repo/...` shape as the original hardcoded string. It proved nothing new.

**The deeper issue:** `pathToFileURL()` in Node.js always uses the *current process's* platform semantics. It cannot be forced to emit a Windows-style `file:///C:/...` URL while running on a POSIX process (or vice versa). Since `resolveCodeModeWorkerUrl()` itself is platform-dependent internally (`fileURLToPath()` + `path.sep`), **no single-platform CI run can fully simulate a different platform's path semantics for this function** — that limitation is real and I cannot test around it without an actual Windows runner.

## What this version actually verifies

Instead of asserting against a hardcoded string, the test now asserts the *contract* of `resolveCodeModeWorkerUrl()`:

1. A module URL whose path contains a `dist` segment resolves to `<distRoot>/agents/code-mode.worker.js`
2. A hashed `selection-*.js` module resolves to the **same destination** as the stable `code-mode.js` module

Both assertions are built using the *current process's own* `pathToFileURL`/`path.join`, so the test is internally consistent on whichever platform CI actually runs (Linux today, and correctly so if this ever runs on a Windows runner) — rather than a string that only ever incidentally matched Linux.

## Behavioral proof

Verified the new fixture against a standalone reimplementation of `resolveCodeModeWorkerUrl`'s exact `distMarker` lookup logic (copied directly from `code-mode.ts`):

```
$ node -e "... (full distMarker/path.join logic from code-mode.ts) ..."
workerUrl:      file:///cwd/dist-test-fixture/dist/agents/code-mode.worker.js
stableResolved: file:///cwd/dist-test-fixture/dist/agents/code-mode.worker.js
hashedResolved: file:///cwd/dist-test-fixture/dist/agents/code-mode.worker.js
stable match: true
hashed match: true
```

I was not able to run the full `vitest` suite in my sandbox — `vitest.config.ts` requires the complete monorepo dependency graph, which isn't installed in this environment — but the logic verified above is the exact `distMarker`/`path.join` algorithm from `resolveCodeModeWorkerUrl` in `code-mode.ts`, not an approximation.

## Honest limitation

This test cannot prove the function behaves correctly *specifically on Windows* — that would require an actual Windows CI runner or a maintainer with Windows access to confirm. What it does prove is that the test itself no longer silently passes by coincidence on Linux while claiming a cross-platform guarantee it can't back up.

## Changes

```
src/agents/code-mode.test.ts — 1 file, test-only
```

target.title: fix(context-engine): bound deferred turn maintenance with a per-task timeout (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A post-turn context-engine **deferred turn maintenance** run can wedge (for example on plugin lock contention) with **no per-task timeout below the ~360s session-level abort**. While it is wedged it holds its maintenance lane, so a user message queued behind it on the same session can wait minutes before being processed, even though the model is healthy. `session.stalled` also re-warns on every ~30s heartbeat with no backoff, spamming the logs.

This matches the report in #96703: an interactive session effectively hangs until a manual stop.

## Why This Change Was Made

Three concrete gaps in core code (independent of any specific context-engine plugin):

1. **No per-task timeout was available on the maintenance enqueue.** `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance` enqueued the maintenance worker via `enqueueCommandInLane(...)` with no options. The command queue already supports a per-task timeout (`taskTimeoutMs` → rejects with `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` and releases the lane), but the maintenance lane never wired it up, so an operator hitting the wedge had no knob below the session-level abort.
2. **A timed-out maintenance worker was not fenced.** Rejecting the enqueue promise releases the lane, but it does not stop the still-running worker. A wedged worker could later call `runtimeContext.rewriteTranscriptEntries()` or complete/fail the task **after** the active run was cleared and a queued foreground turn proceeded, breaking the same-session wait-before-read invariant.
3. **`session.stalled` had no log backoff.** `session.stuck` and `session.long_running` already throttle repeats; `session.stalled` did not.

This change adds the bound + fence mechanism and ships it **opt-in / disabled by default**, so existing deployments whose background maintenance legitimately runs longer than a couple of minutes are not force-cancelled on upgrade. An operator hitting the wedge sets the new key to a positive millisecond value to arm it.

## User Impact

- **No behavior change on upgrade unless configured.** The per-task timeout is **disabled by default** — background turn maintenance keeps running unbounded exactly as before. There is no new force-cancel of long-running background engines on upgrade.
- Operators experiencing the wedge **opt in** by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` to a positive millisecond value (absent or `0` keeps it disabled). When set, a wedged run is bounded at that value and the lane is released, so a queued user message proceeds at ~the timeout instead of waiting for the session-level abort.
- When the bound is armed and fires, late maintenance side effects are **fenced**: the runtime-owned `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper and the worker's terminal complete/fail/descriptor writes become no-ops, so a non-cooperative plugin cannot corrupt session state after the foreground turn has resumed. When the bound is disabled, the fence is never armed.
- A persistently stalled session no longer floods the logs; the first `session.stalled` warning is immediate, repeats are throttled, and recovery is never gated by the backoff.

### Changes

- `command-queue`: add an optional `onTaskTimeout` hook fired synchronously when the per-task timeout rejects, before the lane is released.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: resolve an **opt-in** per-task timeout from `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` (absent / `0` / non-positive ⇒ no bound, no fence); when a positive value is set, pass `taskTimeoutMs` + `onTaskTimeout` to the deferred maintenance enqueue and trip a per-run fence on timeout (hook + defensive trip in the timeout catch); thread the fence into the worker and the runtime-context `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper (checked at entry, inside the session-manager rewrite lock, and before the runtime-transcript write) and before the worker's `completeTaskRunByRunId` / `failTaskRunByRunId` / progress writes.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: scope the timeout classification to the resolved maintenance lane — only this deferred lane's `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` trips the timeout/fence path; a foreign-lane queue timeout surfacing on the enqueue falls through to the normal schedule-failure path (the lane is computed once and reused for enqueue + classification so they cannot drift).
- `session.stalled`: apply the existing `session.stuck`/`session.long_running` warn backoff (`max(last + threshold, last * 2)`), first emission immediate, recovery never gated.
- Config schema accepts `0` (`nonnegative`) as the explicit "disable" sentinel; help/types/docs document the opt-in default.
- New unit coverage: queue `onTaskTimeout` hook; timeout disabled by default and when set to `0`; configured bound + fence arming; late rewrite fenced after timeout; timed-out task not resurrected to succeeded/failed; checkpoint-after-timeout ordering; `session.stalled` backoff.

## Evidence

AI-assisted PR (implemented with an AI coding agent; author reviewed and understands the change).

**Static / unit validation** (this branch):
- `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:test:src` — clean
- `command-queue.test.ts` — 34 passed; `context-engine-maintenance.test.ts` — 60 passed (incl. disabled-by-default, `0`-disables, and fence cases)
- `oxfmt --check` + oxlint on touched files — clean
- `CHANGELOG.md` not touched (left for maintainers at landing)

**Real runtime reproduction (end-to-end, in a container).** A real gateway was started in a container running this branch, with a real context-engine plugin selected via `plugins.slots.contextEngine` whose `info.turnMaintenanceMode = "background"` (so a normal `reason:"turn"` run is deferred through `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance`). Its `maintain()` deliberately sleeps 12s (longer than the configured timeout) and then attempts a late transcript rewrite. The bound was **explicitly opted in** for the test: `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs = 5000`. Two real agent turns were run on one session. Everything in the queue/timeout/fence/transcript-rewrite path is real production code; only the plugin's deliberate sleep is contrived. Logs redacted (provider/model/host/ids replaced with placeholders):

```
[T+0.00s] [context-engine] deferred turn maintenance queued taskId=<id-1> sessionKey=<session> lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session>
[T+0.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 START                       # deferred maintenance begins, will sleep 12s
[T+2.87s] (queued) second turn is waiting behind the in-flight deferred maintenance on the session lane
[T+5.00s] [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session> durationMs=5000 error="CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError: ... task timed out after 5000ms"
[T+5.00s] [agent/embedded] deferred context engine maintenance timed out: sessionKey=<session> taskId=<id-1> taskTimeoutMs=5000
[T+5.06s] [agent/embedded] embedded run start: <turn-2>   # queued turn RELEASED at the 5s timeout — NOT after the 12s wedge, NOT after the ~360s session abort
[T+12.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 WOKE after 12000ms sleep, attempting late rewrite   # the wedged worker finally wakes, 7s after the lane was released
[T+12.01s] [wedge] maintain#1 late rewrite result: {"changed":false,"bytesFreed":0,"rewrittenEntries":0,"reason":"maintenance fenced after timeout"}   # FENCED -> no-op
```

Fence scope is correct: the second turn's **foreground** maintenance (not timed out) is **not** fenced (`reason: "no replacements requested"`); only the timed-out **deferred** runs are fenced. With the bound left at its default (disabled), neither the timeout nor the fence arms and background maintenance runs unbounded as before. A scripted reproduction is available on request.

Closes #96703

target.title: fix(cli): avoid false local gateway unreachable on probe timeout
target.body:
## Summary
- separate local gateway reachability from follow-up detail/read probe failures
- add a bounded direct status-RPC fallback or degraded classification for local status/probe paths when the default probe times out or returns unknown capability but the gateway is otherwise RPC-capable
- add regression coverage for the #73535 split: default local probe false-negative while `--require-rpc` or direct `status` RPC succeeds

## Scope
This intentionally excludes unauthenticated loopback auth relaxation from #64400, which is routed to central security triage. The fix should stay in the ordinary status/probe classification and fallback path.

## Credit

target.title: test(plugins): cover filtered bundle MCP servers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR tightens the existing compatible-bundle MCP path for bundles that need a narrower default tool surface.

- Adds regression coverage that a Codex-compatible bundle `.mcp.json` preserves `toolFilter.include`, `toolFilter.exclude`, and a relative bundle data root.
- Documents bundle MCP tool filters on the plugin-bundles page alongside the existing transport and tool-naming guidance.
- Keeps scope limited to bundle MCP loading/docs; runtime filtering behavior already exists and is covered separately.

Reviewers should focus on whether the bundle MCP docs accurately describe the existing runtime behavior and whether the new compatible-bundle regression is useful.

## Linked context

No issue filed.

Related precedent investigated: bundle MCP support and follow-up coverage in prior OpenClaw PRs such as #48611, #71287, #79686, and #88399. This PR follows that existing bundle MCP seam.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Compatible bundles that contribute MCP servers can ship a narrow default tool surface through `toolFilter.include` and `toolFilter.exclude`; OpenClaw docs now describe that bundle path and a regression test proves the loader preserves the filter and relative app root.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `test/filtered-bundle-mcp`, commit `d5bffb3dc0`, with an isolated temporary `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary Codex-compatible bundle containing `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, `.mcp.json`, `skills/`, and `apps/`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins install --link <temp>/vision-mcp`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins inspect vision-mcp --json`; then loaded bundle MCP config with `node --import tsx` calling `loadEnabledBundleMcpConfig(...)` against the isolated home/config.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the temporary real OpenClaw setup:

```text
Proof root: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install --link /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Linked plugin path: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Restart the gateway to load plugins.
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect vision-mcp --json
{
  "diagnostics": [],
  "command": "npx",
  "args": [
    "-y",
    "@vision-mcp/cli@latest",
    "serve",
    "--apps-root",
    "/private/var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp/apps"
  ],
  "cwdEndsWith": true,
  "toolFilter": {
    "include": [
      "capsule.*",
      "vision_map.snapshot",
      "vision_map.list_apps",
      "vision_map.list_workflows",
      "vision_map.describe_workflow",
      "vision_map.describe",
      "vision_map.detect_state",
      "vision_map.list_actions",
      "vision_map.describe_action",
      "vision_map.perform_action",
      "vision_map.run_workflow",
      "vision_map.verify",
      "vision_map.repair_minimal",
      "vision_map.export_trace"
    ],
    "exclude": [
      "vision_map.click_at",
      "vision_map.type_text",
      "vision_map.press_key",
      "vision_map.scroll",
      "vision_map.init",
      "vision_map.apply_patch",
      "vision_map.add_control",
      "vision_map.commit_*",
      "vision_map.harvest_session"
    ]
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw linked the temporary compatible bundle, detected its bundle MCP server, preserved both include and exclude filter arrays, set `cwd` to the bundle root, and resolved `./apps` to an absolute path inside the installed bundle.

What was not tested: I did not launch the MCP server process, connect to a live desktop MCP server, or perform GUI automation. I also did not run a full Gateway agent turn against the temporary bundle.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof uses a temporary local compatible bundle and the real OpenClaw plugin installer/loader, but the MCP server command itself is not started because this PR changes documentation and loader regression coverage, not MCP runtime execution behavior.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs -- src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts --reporter=verbose
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm check:docs
codex review --base origin/main
```

Regression coverage added: `src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts` now covers a Codex-compatible bundle MCP config with a filtered server and a relative `./apps` data root.

Validation results:

```text
src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: 1 file passed, 8 tests passed
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: All matched files use the correct format
git diff --check: passed
pnpm check:docs: Docs formatting clean; markdownlint 0 errors; MDX check passed; i18n glossary passed; checked_internal_links=4554; broken_links=0
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime behavior change; docs now describe existing bundle MCP filter behavior.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime change. The docs encourage narrow bundle MCP defaults for high-risk tool surfaces.

What is the highest-risk area? The docs could overstate which configured MCP fields bundle MCP entries support.

How is that risk mitigated? Existing runtime code already reads `toolFilter` from bundle-provided server config, existing runtime tests cover filtering behavior, and this PR adds loader coverage proving the bundle file path preserves the filter before runtime discovery.

## Current review state

AI-assisted: yes.

Next action: maintainer review.

target.title: fix(mattermost): bound null-body error response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`readMattermostError()` had a `!res.body` fallback that bypassed the bounded reader and called native `res.json()` / `res.text()` directly. For a null-body error response with a JSON content-type (a 401/503 with `content-type: application/json` and an empty body — common from reverse proxies and gateways), `await res.json()` throws `SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input`. That rejection propagates out of `readMattermostError()`, so the caller's intended `Mattermost API <status>: unknown error` is replaced by a confusing JSON-parse crash. It was also the last remaining unbounded `json()` / `text()` read in this error path after #96033 bounded the success path.

Affected surface: `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts` — `readMattermostError()`, reached by `client.request()`, the file-upload path, and `probe.ts`.

## Why This Change Was Made

The `!res.body` special-case is removed entirely, so every error read goes through the existing bounded `readResponseTextLimited` (8 KiB) and the JSON `message` is parsed from that bounded text. `readResponseTextLimited` returns `""` when there is no readable body, so a null-body response now yields an empty detail instead of throwing — and all three callers already coalesce that to a clean message (`detail || "unknown error"` / `detail || res.statusText`). One canonical read path, no second buffering strategy.

Net change is a deletion (`client.ts` +0/−10): removes the divergent branch rather than adding code.

## User Impact

Operators and agents talking to self-hosted or misbehaving Mattermost servers get a clean, accurate API error (`Mattermost API 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error`) on null-body JSON error responses, instead of an internal `Unexpected end of JSON input` crash — and every error read now goes through the bounded reader.

## Evidence

**1. Real-behavior proof with a negative control (real HTTP).** A harness drives the **real exported** `readMattermostError` and the **real** `createMattermostClient().request()` (through the real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` SSRF path, `allowPrivateNetwork: true` for localhost) against a real `node:http` server returning `503 Service Unavailable` with `content-type: application/json` and **no body**. The negative control runs the deleted branch's exact logic (`await res.json()`) on the same response.

```
$ tsx mm-proof.mts
Server returns 503 Service Unavailable, content-type application/json, NO body

[NEGATIVE CONTROL] old `!res.body` json branch: await res.json()
PASS: old branch crashes on null-body JSON error — THREW SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input

[WITH FIX] readMattermostError(real null-body 503 json response)
PASS: returns empty string, no throw — detail=""

[WITH FIX] createMattermostClient().request() against null-body 503
PASS: clean bounded API error (no JSON crash) — "Mattermost API 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error"

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

Without the fix the null-body JSON error path throws `SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input`; with the fix `readMattermostError` returns `""` and the client surfaces the clean `... 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error`.

**2. Committed regression tests** (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.test.ts`) cover: null-body JSON errors never call `response.json()` / `response.text()`; bounded JSON `message` fields still parse from normal bodies; and a guarded null-body failure surfaced end-to-end through `createMattermostClient().request()`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  27 passed (27)
```

**3. Lint on the changed file:**

```
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts   # exit 0
```

**What was NOT tested:** I did not run against a live production Mattermost server. The null-body JSON error condition is reproduced with a real local HTTP server over the real SSRF-guarded fetch path, including the negative control showing the pre-fix crash.

AI-assisted: implementation, tests, and the proof harness were drafted with agent assistance; all commands above were run locally.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`
